AWD · Quick Guide

Handling User Input and Data Conversion

← Back to Course Package

Key Concepts

  • The Input Pipeline: A professional four-phase workflow (Capture, Convert, Validate, Format) that transforms unpredictable user data into reliable application state.
  • ES Modules (ESM): The modern JavaScript standard using import and export, required for top-level await and modern library compatibility.
  • Transpilation: The essential step of converting TypeScript source code into executable JavaScript via the tsc compiler.
  • Type Safety: The practice of ensuring user-provided strings are explicitly converted and checked before being used in logic to prevent runtime crashes.
  • Node.js Runtime: The environment where your compiled code executes, requiring specific type definitions (@types/node) to understand terminal commands.

Syntax / API Reference

1. Project Configuration (package.json)
Enable modern modules to use await at the top level:

"type": "module"

2. Importing the Readline Interface
Use the promise-based version for cleaner asynchronous code:

import * as readline from 'node:readline/promises';
import { stdin as input, stdout as output } from 'node:process';

3. Capturing Input
Create the interface and prompt the user:

const rl = readline.createInterface({ input, output });
const answer = await rl.question('Enter a number: ');
rl.close();

4. Conversion and Validation
Transform the string and check for errors:

const num = parseFloat(answer);
if (isNaN(num)) { console.error('Invalid input'); }

Step-by-Step Workflow

  1. Initialize Project: Run npm init -y and set "type": "module" in your package.json.
  2. Install Dependencies: Run npm install --save-dev typescript @types/node to get the compiler and Node.js definitions.
  3. Configure TypeScript: Generate your config file using npx tsc --init.
  4. Implement Logic: Create your .ts file and apply the Capture (readline), Convert (parseFloat/parseInt), Validate (isNaN check), and Format (rounding or template literals) phases.
  5. Build and Run: Compile your code with npx tsc and execute the resulting file with node dist/index.js (or your specific output path).

Gotcha Alerts

  • ⚠️ Missing Node Types: If you don't install @types/node, TypeScript will claim it cannot find process or readline.
  • ⚠️ Hanging Terminals: Forgetting to call rl.close() will keep the Node process running indefinitely after your code finishes.
  • ⚠️ Partial Parsing: parseFloat("10abc") returns 10 rather than NaN; use strict validation if you need to reject mixed strings.
  • ⚠️ Module Mismatch: The await keyword will throw a syntax error if your package.json is not set to "type": "module".

Key Takeaways

  • Establish a repeatable 4-phase pipeline to handle the 'Wild West' of user input safely.
  • Configure a modern TypeScript environment using ES Modules and proper type definitions.
  • Utilize the readline/promises module to create interactive command-line applications.
  • Protect your application logic by validating data immediately after conversion.
